title: US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium – US politics live
summary: US energy secretary Chris Wright says pair of agreements signed Wednesday ‘uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation’
body: At a Donald Trump event in Marietta, Georgia, the state’s Republican candidate for governor Rick Jackson pledged to ask the Georgia legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts, and if they failed to do so, would match the figure himself. “Here in Georgia, we should not stope with $1000,” Jackson said. “I believe our legislature will approve that. If they don’t I will personally donate it myself.” Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3 billion. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded a bit more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126 million in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15 billion in the bank, with $9 billion in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia, which borders Yemen, led a military intervention in 2015, triggering a year-long civil war to prop up Yemen’s Saudi-backed government. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations Centre said in an alert issued on Wednesday night it “has received a report of an incident 70NM southwest of AL SHUQAIQ, Saudi Arabia. The Master of a tanker reports being struck by an unknown projectile which has caused a fire onboard that the crew are currently fighting.” Senator Chris Murphy, a Connecticut Democrat, responded to news of the Houthi attacks by writing on social media: “Everyone saw all of this coming. This chaos was totally foreseeable and Trump chose to start this war anyway.” During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia on Wednesday, Donald Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff, the incumbent Democratic senator whose withering criticism of the president has thrilled Democrats and led to calls for Ossoff to mount a 2028 presidential campaign, should he first win his reelection bid in November. “I fought Ossoff before, as you know, and we won. We had a great success against this guy,” Trump said, fondly recalling his role in Ossoff’s narrow loss in a 2017 special election in Georgia’s sixth congressional district, in what was then a Republican-dominated Atlanta suburb. “He reminds me of Pinky Herman,” the president said. “Do you ever see Pinky Herman?” Trump asked, a comment that prompted some laughter from his supporters in the hall, and a scramble, among journalists, to figure out what, exactly, he was talking about. It was not until fully thirteen minutes later, when Trump predicted that Ossoff’s Republican challenger, Mike Collins, is “going to beat Pee-wee Herman”, that the 80-year-old president’s reference to the character played by the late comic actor Paul Reubens four decades ago became clear. For a president who rose to power largely through his insult-comic hits on his political opponents, it was a peculiarly low-energy jibe, given that Ossoff, who was once as skinny as the Reubens character, has now bulked up, and speaks about the president in a tone of righteous indignation that bears no resemblance to Pee-wee’s squeaky voice. Trump made no mention at all of the prominent role he played in trying, and failing, to stop Ossoff from winning his Senate seat in a run-off election on 5 January, 2021. The night before that election, in which Democratic senator Raphael Warnock also won his seat, Trump had traveled to Georgia to speak at a rally for the two Republican candidates, senators Kelly Loeffler and David Perdue. Rather than urging voters to support the Republican candidates, Trump instead undermined confidence in the electoral system in remarks focused mainly on his claim that the presidential election just two months earlier had been stolen from him. “Hello, Georgia! By the way, there is no way we lost Georgia. There’s no way, … That was a rigged election” Trump began his speech that night. “When you win in a landslide, and they steal it, and it’s rigged, it’s not acceptable,” he told the crowd, hours before Republican turnout slumped and Ossoff and Warnock were elected, swinging control of the Senate to the Democrats. The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with nearly all Democrats opposing its passage. The measure now goes to the Senate. Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military. “For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote. But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed. Donald Trump is currently delivering what is supposedly a presidential address to promote a new domestic policy, namely the investment accounts for children he calls Trump accounts, but, surrounded by supporters in Marietta, Georgia, his remarks quickly became deeply partisan, repeatedly attacking Democrats and falsely claiming the party is now filled with “communists”. Trump, whose political mentor Roy Cohn led senator Joseph McCarthy’s 1950s witch hunts for imaginary communist infiltrators in the US government, made the wild claim that Democrats he called “communists by another name” want to blow up Mount Rushmore. He then proclaimed: “we declare again today that the United States of America will never be a communist country!” The crowd, led by a group of die-hard Trump supporters who call themselves Front Row Joes, for their repeated presence at his rallies, broke into a chant of “USA! USA!” As I noted earlier, the parents of Lt Tyler Feehan accompanied Trump from Dover to Georgia aboard Air Force One, after the president attended a dignified transfer for four service members killed in the Middle East. Trump addressed Feehan’s family and their loss in his remarks today. “[Feehan] was a person that was going all the way. He was going to be a general. He would have been a general very quickly. He was heading in that path,” Trump said. Donald Trump has started speaking in Marietta, Georgia. He started his speech joking that he was announces his candidacy for another term in office. Despite insisting that he was only “kidding”, it is something that the president has routinely floated – even though a third term would be…

title: Saudi Arabia and US strike deal for kingdom to enrich uranium
summary: Donald Trump approves agreement to help country make fuel for civilian nuclear programme
body: Donald Trump signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactions. The US energy secretary, Chris Wright, and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement,” according to a statement from the US Department of Energy. The timing of the announcement is controversial, as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Saudi Arabia has in the past said it would need a nuclear weapon if Iran acquired one, an intention Iran has repeatedly denied. But in lengthy talks stretching over more than one administration, the US also feared that Riyadh would look elsewhere for nuclear technology if the US did not provide what it wanted. It is likely the agreement will not include the enhanced monitoring by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) that is found in the so-called additional protocol to which Iran had been subjected under the 2015 nuclear deal. Rosemary Kelanic, the head of the Middle East programme at Defense Priorities, warned in a social media post: “Giving Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment technology is a terrible idea on many counts. But it’s especially idiotic to do it *in the middle of a war over Iran’s nuclear programme*.” She described the decision as reckless and not in the US national interest. “Saudi enrichment would put immense pressure on Iran to get the bomb. Is Trump TRYING to provoke Iran into weaponising?” Others said it showed the US thought Saudi Arabia had matured into a country that wanted to diversify its energy mix away from oil. The debate about the wisdom of Iran’s stated refusal to acquire nuclear weapons is very live inside Iran, and there will be anger that Tehran’s biggest regional rival is permitted to enrich uranium domestically while Iran is sanctioned for doing so. In negotiations in Geneva in February, before the outbreak of the war, the US demanded Iran commit to a 10- to 20-year suspension of any right to enrich domestically. Iran was willing to consider a suspension of three years. Separately, the US had been willing to consider a regional consortium to enrich uranium that would include Saudi Arabia, Iran, the US and the United Arab Emirates. Saudi Arabia is a member state of the IAEA, a Vienna-based UN agency that promotes peaceful nuclear work but also does inspections to make sure countries do not have clandestine atomic weapons programmes. Congress can review and block nuclear cooperation agreements, also known as “123 agreements”. Enrichment to 90% purity is only one step in the process of acquiring a nuclear weapon. Saudi Arabia and nuclear-armed Pakistan signed a mutual defence pact after Israel launched an attack on Qatar targeting Hamas officials. Pakistan’s defence minister then said his nation’s nuclear programme “will be made available” to Saudi Arabia if needed, something seen as a warning for Israel, long known as the Middle East’s only nuclear-armed state. The United Arab Emirates, a neighbour to Saudi Arabia, signed a 123 agreement with the US to build its Barakah nuclear power plant with South Korean assistance. But the UAE did so without seeking enrichment, something non-proliferation experts have held up as the “gold standard” for countries wanting nuclear power. During Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s visit to Washington in November, the White House announced a US-Saudi framework for nuclear cooperation that “builds the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar nuclear energy partnership” between the two countries “in a manner consistent with strong nonproliferation standards”. In a report to Congress earlier this year, the US administration urged support for the US-Saudi nuclear deal, in part to “prevent strategic competition from seizing an opportunity to undermine United States national security interests for decades to come”. The report also argued that the US must seize the opportunity to expand nuclear cooperation to “re-establish our leadership in the global civilian nuclear energy market and reap the benefits of expanded influence in foreign countries hosting our reactors”.

title: Scottish Labour’s rivals decry ‘chancer’ Anas Sarwar for resigning to join Andy Burnham’s government
summary: New prime minister has made Sarwar – the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer’s resignation – a peer
body: Scottish Labour’s rivals in Holyrood have slammed Anas Sarwar’s shock decision to resign as leader to sit in Andy Burnham’s new government, calling him a “chancer” and accusing him of lying to voters. The prime minister has made Sarwar – who was the first senior Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer to resign – a peer and given him a ministerial post at the Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, No 10 announced on Wednesday. The Scottish Green party co-leader Gillian Mackay said: “Anas Sarwar is a chancer. He has gone from promising to scrap the House of Lords to taking a seat in it. He has spent years attacking the very institution he is now preparing to join. The hypocrisy is breathtaking.” The SNP called for Sarwar to donate his new “bumper salary” to charity, accusing him of lying to the electorate over his intentions after previously saying he would never enter the Lords, calling it “unacceptable” in 2022. The SNP MSP George Adam said: “The Labour party and Anas Sarwar repeatedly denied and lied to the people of Scotland about Anas Sarwar’s intentions – the fact he will now be prancing about in ermine robes won’t be forgotten.” However, despite the move happening less than three months since his election campaign – shortly before Scottish Labour suffered its worst results in Holyrood history – those who know Sarwar do not think it was planned. Figures from within his own party were left reeling, with “surprised” being the word on the lips of a number of Scottish Labour politicians and staff on Wednesday. One Scottish Labour worker said: “If he was going to resign, it would have been clearer to have stated that in May and given everyone the chance to have a proper assessment and restructure things however needed. Doing it this way means everyone is thrown into the lurch.” As is the formality in some constituencies, Sarwar’s seat as a member of the Scottish parliament will be relinquished and handed on to the next on the list, Monica Lennon. Lennon is also seen as a credible contender for the party leadership, having been a popular MSP in the past. Michael Marra, the MSP for North East Scotland, is a name also being put forward as a potential leadership candidate from outside the power centres of Edinburgh or Glasgow. It will not be a wide field, as there are only 17 Labour MSPs to choose from. It is possible for an MP or even a Lord to take on the Scottish Labour leadership – meaning, in fact, Sarwar does not have to technically resign as leader – but it is most common for this role to be held by an MSP because of duties in the Scottish parliament. A source close to Sarwar said: “The next [Scottish] Labour leader will need to [provide] balance between the offer to Scotland that is a uniquely Scottish offer, while being part of a UK-wide party. That has been a constant conflict, a constant battle.” Another said the next leader would “have to do some educating of Andy Burnham … He’s the king of the north but he is prime minister of the United Kingdom.” Criticism has been made from Scotland that Burnham’s devolution offer to the north of England and the wider country outside London and the south-east falls short when it comes to the devolved nations. Scottish politicians from across the spectrum have complained that Burnham lacks knowledge of, or interest in, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. Sarwar’s appointment could help bridge that gap, a source said. While many people were shocked by Sarwar’s perceived willingness to leave Scotland behind, given the choice between being in power in Westminster and in opposition in Holyrood, it would be common sense for Sarwar to choose Westminster, a source close to Sarwar in the Labour party said. “I’m maybe less surprised than people out in the wider Labour world, because he’s someone who’s very ambitious. Politically, he’s someone who wants to effect change. He’s obviously not in power, he faced the prospect of hanging around for another five years until the next Holyrood election. I just think we would have been bored had he stayed on, and he’s not someone that would like to sit around and be bored.” Sarwar said: “I am delighted and honoured to have been asked by the prime minister to serve every part of the United Kingdom as the minister of state for trade. “Throughout my life, I have only ever been motivated by public service. The opportunity to serve our country is all I have ever asked. That’s why this is an opportunity I humbly accept. “Looking back over the last five years, I am hugely proud of the work we have done since I became Scottish Labour leader.” The acting leader of the Scottish Labour party, Jackie Baillie, said: “I want to thank Anas Sarwar for his leadership and friendship. He led the Scottish Labour party with integrity and a vision to build a fairer and more equal Scotland.”

title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
summary: Trump energy secretary hails agreement that could potentially allow kingdom to enrich uranium
body: The US has signed a landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could potentially allow the kingdom to enrich uranium. In a statement on Wednesday, the US Department of Energy said its secretary, Chris Wright, and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed a “peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement”, along with a “bilateral safeguards agreement”. According to the department, the partnership is expected to expand American nuclear technology exports, create “high-paying” US jobs, and “strengthen America’s energy and national security posture”. “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening US-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States. Thanks to President Trump, the American nuclear renaissance is under way and will deliver long-term benefits to the American and Saudi people.” The deal, which the energy department said would also help “meet Saudi energy needs”, will now be transmitted to Congress for review. It could face pushback from lawmakers concerned about how the deal will be monitored. In a statement to CBS, Dan Sumner, chief executive of Westinghouse Electric Company – an American nuclear reactor company expected to support Saudi Arabia’s nuclear development – said: “The US government’s announcement to launch a civil nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia is a landmark step that strengthens energy security, expands opportunities for US industry and workers, and drives significant long-term economic growth and investment benefits for both countries.” Although the full details of the deal have yet to be released, it comes at a controversial time, as the US remains engaged in its war with Iran – a conflict that the defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, says has cost $37.5bn so far. Just a day earlier, Hegseth appeared before the US Senate to request additional funding from the Senate appropriations committee, as the US continues its campaign to prevent Iran from developing uranium enrichment capabilities and obtaining a nuclear weapon.

title: Iran warns of ‘eye for an eye’ response if US follows through on Trump’s threats to destroy infrastructure – as it happened
summary: US president vows to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for each strait of Hormuz attack; Iran’s military says it will stop Gulf oil flow and target regional infrastructure if US acts on threats, state tv reports
body: The Houthis ⁠- the Iran-backed group in Yemen, said on Thursday they had carried ⁠out ⁠a military operation targeting two Saudi ⁠oil tankers, identified as ENCELA and ⁠LAYLIA, saying the vessels violated ‌the ‌naval blockade imposed ‌by the group on Monday. The Houthis’ announcement prompted the Saudi-led coalition to declare that it ⁠would respond “firmly”, in a move that threatens to disrupt global ‌energy supplies beyond the Gulf. The Saudi authorities have not immediately confirmed the strike. The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) said it had received reports that a tanker had been struck by an unknown projectile and caught fire in the Red Sea, 70 nautical miles from Saudi Arabia’s Al Shuqaiq. At the time of writing there are no reported casualties and the projectile’s origin has not been specified. Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi said that Tehran would respond in kind to any attack on its infrastructure, after Donald Trump’s threat to bomb a bridge or power plant for every ship targeted in the strait of Hormuz. “Our defence doctrine is clear: eye for an eye. Any aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response,” Araghchi said on X. Iran’s state broadcaster also quoted the Iranian military as saying that US threats “will have no result other than the expansion of the war”. It added that if the US acts on the threats, Iran will stop all Gulf oil flow and target oil, gas, electricity and economic infrastructure in the region. Iran’s top negotiator and powerful parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, also warned on Wednesday that if Tehran cannot export its oil, no other country in the region would be able to do so. He wrote on X: “The equation of this war is clear: either all or none! In a region where we do not sell oil, no one will sell oil. If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe, and the security of the strait is in the absence of American forces. We have repeatedly said that the situation of the strait will not return to pre-war conditions. He also repeated that the strait of Hormuz “will not return to pre-war conditions”, as he has warned several times before. It all came after Donald Trump threatened retribution against Iran in a post on Truth Social on Wednesday. “From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran,” the US president said on Truth Social. The threat came after the US conducted its 11th consecutive day of strikes on Iran, including on aircraft hangars and drone storage sites, as the renewed conflict continues to undermine diplomatic efforts to salvage an interim ceasefire deal. Trump also attended the dignified transfer of four US service members killed in the Middle East over the weekend, the third such ceremony the US president has attended since he launched the war in Iran in February. Speaking to reporters on his way to Dover, Delaware, Trump called it “one of the hardest things to do as a president”. His message to grieving families, he said, will simply be “we love you, we love your child”. The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US energy secretary Chris Wright said. As my colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier, the timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among US lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Fatemeh Aman, an Iran expert and a former non-resident fellow at the Middle East Institute and a former senior fellow at the Atlantic Council, told the Guardian: Iran is trying to balance deterrence with restraint. It wants to demonstrate that US military action carries costs while avoiding a full-scale war that could threaten the survival of the regime. Tehran therefore favours calibrated retaliation, enough to preserve credibility, but not enough to trigger overwhelming US escalation. A drawn-out war would be costly for both sides, but probably more so for Iran. The United States has greater military and economic resources, while Iran faces growing pressure on its economy, infrastructure, and domestic stability. That is why Tehran’s objective is to raise the costs for Washington, not to sustain an open-ended conflict. Iran is already using missiles and drones against US bases and targets in Gulf states, while also attacking shipping and disrupting traffic through the strait of Hormuz. The most likely option is therefore a continuation of these attacks possibly with changes in intensity and target selection. Iran can also expand cyberoperations, covert attacks, and pressure through allied armed groups. The more escalatory options would be larger attacks on Persian Gulf energy infrastructure or attempts to cause more sustained disruption to regional shipping. However, these would risk provoking an even broader US response and further alienating Gulf governments. Iran still has the ability to impose significant regional and economic costs, but its capacity to sustain a prolonged war of attrition is more limited than that of the United States. Such a conflict would be damaging to both sides, but probably more damaging to Iran because of the imbalance in military resources and Iran’s greater economic and infrastructural vulnerability. According to Aman, in the short term, the attacks are likely to strengthen the security establishment. Hardliners can argue that negotiations alone cannot guarantee Iran’s security and that military deterrence remains essential. Over time, however, the conflict could expose deep divisions within the leadership over the costs of continued confrontation. If economic conditions worsen and military losses mount, debates over strategy are likely to become more pronounced, even if they remain largely behind closed doors. The strikes are likely to delay parts of Iran’s nuclear program by damaging facilities, equipment, and infrastructure. However, they are unlikely to eliminate the program altogether. Iran retains technical expertise, scientific knowledge, and the ability to rebuild over time. Military action can slow the program, but it cannot permanently remove the underlying capability or motivation. Without a political agreement and an effective inspection regime, the strikes are more likely to buy time than provide a lasting solution. Iran’s foreign ministry has said it is “shameful” that US lawmakers didn’t discuss the human suffering caused by the US-Israeli war on his country during a congressional hearing on additional funding for the conflict on Wednesday. Esmaeil Baqaei wrote on X: It is shameful that during the U.S. Congress hearing regarding the performance of the U.S. Secretary of War in the war against the Iranian nation, not even a single question was raised about the human dimensions of this brutal war, despite the fact that all of them are well aware that the U.S. military aggression against Iran has been an illegal war accompanied by countless war crimes, including the crimes of #Minab and #Lamard. As you will remember, at least 175 people – most of them children – were killed on the first day of the war when a Tomahawk missile hit a girls’ elementary school in the southern Iranian city of Minab. A prelimin…

title: House Republicans to write $95bn bill to fund Iran war and aid farmers
summary: GOP-controlled chamber paves way for reconciliation bill, while making long-shot attempt at new voting restrictions
body: House Republicans on Wednesday agreed to begin the process of writing a $95bn bill to fund the war with Iran as well as provide new aid to farmers, while making a long-shot attempt to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. In a virtually party-line 216-214, the Republican-controlled chamber passed a budget resolution that paves the way for the creation of a reconciliation bill, which can advance through the Senate under special rules that circumvent the Democratic filibuster and requires only a simple majority to pass. Two Republicans – Warren Davidson of Ohio and Thomas Massie of Kentucky, as well as Kevin Kiley, an independent of California – joined all Democrats in opposing the measure. The resolution’s passage comes amid an intensification of the conflict with Iran, which Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, told the Senate on Tuesday had thus far cost $37.5bn. The Trump administration last month asked Congress to authorize approximately $88bn in supplemental funding, the majority of which – up to $67.1bn – will go to pay for the war. The budget resolution passed by the House largely answers that call by authorizing the armed services committee to propose $60bn in spending for the fiscal years through 2036, and the intelligence committee an additional $13bn. The $73bn sum is expected to go towards military operations against Iran. The agriculture committee has been allocated $12bn in spending changes, which it is expected to propose using on measures to help farmers rocked by the global effects of the conflict with Iran, as well as Trump’s tariff regimen against major trading partners. The House administration committee authorized a further $10bn, which is set to be used to make an unlikely effort to pass the Save America Act into law. The measure, championed by Donald Trump, would ban mail-in ballots nationwide and impose enhanced strict identification requirements on voters when they cast ballots and register. But legislation seeking to advance through the Senate under the rules of budget reconciliation can only deal with spending, revenue and the debt limit, making it likely that the Save America Act’s policy changes will be stripped out when the chamber takes up the measure. Mike Johnson nodded to that fact on Tuesday, when he told reporters the goal of the bill was to pass the Save America Act into law “as much of that as possible”. “As always, Republicans support our troops, our farmers and election integrity and we will demonstrate that again this week,” the Republican House speaker said. The budget resolution is the third attempt Republicans have made in Trump’s second term to use the party-line reconciliation procedure to enact their agenda. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, passed last year, was a reconciliation bill, as was the measure the GOP approved last month that funded agencies involved in Trump’s mass deportation campaign through the remainder of his term. Democrats blasted the budget proposal, with Hakeem Jeffries saying before the vote: “President Trump and Republicans in this House continue to act indifferent to the economic wellbeing of the American people and to actively do things that are hurting everyday Americans financially, like this reckless and costly war of choice.” “Republican extremists apparently have money for wars, but can’t feed the poor,” the House minority leader added. The House’s approval of the resolution came two days before the chamber recesses until the end of August. But John Thune, the Senate’s Republican majority leader, signaled no urgency in passing the blueprint. At a press conference on Tuesday, he said he would “hold” the measure in case negotiations with Democrats on funding the government break down before the 30 September deadline. If that happens, Thune said, the Senate could repurpose it to pass a stopgap funding measure along party lines. “I hope that’s not necessary,” he said. Even if that scenario were avoided, the budget resolution could face headwinds in the Senate Republican conference, where some lawmakers have blanched because it does not bring total defense spending to the $1.5tn the Trump administration has requested for the 2027 fiscal year. Others have objected to the lack of offsets to the new spending the bill will approve, amid the US’s rising debt load.

title: California toddler bitten by rattlesnake ‘hiding under’ Amazon package
summary: Three-year-old girl landed in ICU after snake bit her while grabbing a package outside her family’s Santa Clarita home
body: A three-year-old girl landed in the intensive care unit earlier this month after a rattlesnake bit her while she was trying to grab an Amazon package. Addy, the young girl, ran out with her mother on 13 July to pick up a package dropped over the gate to their home in Santa Clarita, north of Los Angeles. “Tragically, a southern pacific rattlesnake was hiding under the brown padded envelope and bit Addy’s left hand,” her father, Manny Barajas, wrote in a fundraising campaign note. Addy’s mother, Lynn, took the girl to meet first responders at a nearby grocery store parking lot, who took her to hospital and administered a dose of anti-venom. The hospital then transferred her to another facility with a pediatric ICU, where she received two more doses of anti-venom, according to Barajas. The treatment appears to have worked. “She’s doing great now,” Barajas told KTLA, adding: “She’s still bruised on her left hand where she was bitten. Her spirits are high, her demeanor is great, but she keeps asking why she was bitten by a snake.” Rattlesnake bites have become more common in California this year, with heatwaves since March increasing snake activity. Three people have died this year, which is about two more than usual. The California poison control system has received reports of 277 bites so far this year, according to the Los Angeles Times – almost as many as it normally received in an entire year. Rattlesnakes typically avoid humans, but will bite when they feel threatened, cornered or surprised. California poison control and the Los Angeles county fire department did not immediately return requests for comment.

title: House passes $1.15tn defense bill despite standoff over Israel and Trump’s voter agenda
summary: Passage of annual bill complicated by Trump’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without congressional approval
body: The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with all but six Democrats opposing its passage. Seven House Republicans broke ranks to oppose it. The measure now goes to the Senate. Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military. “For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote. But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed. Democrats also soured on the bill after Mike Johnson, the Republican House speaker, acceded to the demands of rightwing lawmakers and inserted into the legislation the Save America Act, a Trump-backed proposal to ban mail-in ballots and impose new identification restrictions on voters at the polls and when they register. The twin dynamics combined to make the Democrats’ opposition nearly unanimous two days before House lawmakers are scheduled to depart Washington DC for a recess that stretches until the end of August. Another sticking point for Democrats was the measure’s overall cost of $1.15tn, and the plan to slash funding elsewhere in the government a year after Congress’s Republican majorities approved extending an array of lower tax rates indefinitely. “Our country faces threats and DoD has modernization needs, but I cannot and do not support meeting these needs by making massive cuts to critically important domestic programs and slashing taxes,” said Adam Smith, the top Democrat on the House armed services committee. “We need to be more fiscally responsible and DoD needs to become more innovative and efficient in terms of developing capabilities to meet our national security needs in a more cost-effective manner.” Amid a wider backlash within the party over US support for Israel, some Democrats said they opposed the NDAA because of its inclusion of provisions that would deepen cooperation between the two countries’ defense industries. “This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy,” said the progressive congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, who was among the no votes. The House’s passage of the bill came after the chamber considered dozens of amendments. While many were agreed to unanimously, others offered barometers for how far the Republican-controlled chamber was willing to go on the most divisive issues before the parties. A proposal by the Republican Eli Crane to halt all military aid to Ukraine was resoundingly defeated by a 76-350 vote, while an amendment from Republican Lauren Boebert to codify Trump’s executive order banning transgender service members failed by a 212-217 tally. However, the House agreed to amendments from Nancy Mace barring the military health insurer from offering some gender-related care, and banning transgender athletes from girls’ sports at defense department-run schools. Beyond the NDAA, House Republicans are also moving forward with a party-line attempt to authorize $60bn in new military spending, much of which is expected to cover costs related to the war with Iran. But that proposal falls far short of the $350bn the Pentagon originally proposed to bring overall defense spending to $1.5tn for the upcoming fiscal year.

title: Election deniers win Republican primaries in Arizona
summary: Andy Biggs wins nomination to challenge Katie Hobbs as Alex Kolodin won party’s primary for secretary of state
body: Donald Trump-backed candidates in Arizona prevailed in key primary contests on Tuesday, setting up a fall campaign that will test whether Republicans can reverse some of their recent losses in the presidential battleground state. Andy Biggs, the hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump, easily won the Republican nomination to challenge the incumbent Democrat, Governor Katie Hobbs. The race is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country, with control of the governor’s office carrying outsized national significance in a state that became “ground zero” for election denialism after Trump’s narrow loss there in 2020. Biggs, a former chair of the House Freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base. Hobbs, who flipped the seat from red to blue in 2022, ran unopposed. Alex Kolodin, a Republican state representative and attorney who helped lead failed legal efforts to overturn Arizona’s 2020 and 2022 election results, also won the party’s primary to become its secretary of state: Arizona’s top election official. Kolodin will face the Democratic incumbent, Adrian Fontes, in November for the office that will oversee elections in a swing state during the 2028 presidential race. Fontes has cast himself as a bulwark against Trump’s election denialism, while overseeing elections in one of the nation’s most closely watched battleground states. In an all-caps social media post on Wednesday, Trump declared “A COMPLETE SWEEP!!!” “MAGA CANDIDATES WON EVERYTHING LAST NIGHT IN ARIZONA,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. He added: “AFTER DECADES, ARIZONA IS BECOMING A REPUBLICAN STATE.” In fact, Arizona was long a Republican stronghold and only recently transformed into a competitive swing state, fueled by rapid population growth, shifting suburban voting patterns around Phoenix and an influx of college-educated voters. In the decade since Trump’s 2016 election, Arizona Democrats have gained control of the top three statewide offices – governor, attorney general and secretary of state – as well as both US Senate seats. But Trump still narrowly carried Arizona in 2024 after losing the state four years earlier – a shock defeat he unsuccessfully sought to overturn. Throughout the Republican gubernatorial campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered contrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’s hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona. But Republican voters in Arizona, as they have across the country. This year, Republicans are contending with unfavorable national political conditions. Historically, the president’s party loses ground in the midterm elections, and Democrats are hopeful they can seize control of the House – and possibly the Senate, riding a wave of discontent over high gas prices and an unpopular Iran war that have dragged down Trump’s approval ratings. Two toss-up House districts in Arizona are key to that effort. Arizona’s first congressional district, anchored in affluent Scottsdale, has long been a Democratic target as the suburbs shifted away from the Republican party over the past decade. Two years ago, Schweikert won re-election with just under 52% of the vote, narrowly defeating Amish Shah, a former state lawmaker who Democrats appeared poised to renominate on Tuesday. His challenger, the former local news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods, also sought the nomination two years ago. This year, Galán-Woods, the widow of Grant Woods, the former Republican attorney general who publicly broke with his party over Trump, earned the controversial backing of the Democratic congressional campaign committee. Shah will face Republican Jay Feely, a former Arizona Cardinals kicker, in November. Feely faced Joseph Chaplik, a former state lawmaker, and John Trobough, a technology and telecommunications executive, in his party’s primary. In the sixth congressional district, which extends from the suburbs of Tucson to the US-Mexico border, encompassing much of south-eastern Arizona, Democrats hope national headwinds facing the president’s party will dislodge Republican congressman Juan Ciscomani, who is seeking a third term. Democrat JoAnna Mendoza, a Marine Corps veteran, was running unopposed. Meanwhile, the contest to succeed Biggs in the safely Republican fifth congressional district has drawn unusual attention. The Trump-backed Republican nominee, Mark Lamb, the former Pinal county sheriff, spent the closing weeks of the primary campaign confronting allegations of sexual misconduct and abuse of power reported in a multi-part investigation by the Arizona Republic. Prosecutors found no basis for criminal charges, and Trump has reaffirmed his support for Lamb, who has denied wrongdoing. He will face Democrat Elizabeth Lee, a nurse, in the general election. Elsewhere, Republicans chose their nominees for two top state positions that Democrats see as critical to pushing back against the Trump administration. Warren Petersen, president of the Arizona senate, won the Republican primary race to take on Democrat Kris Mayes for attorney general. Mayes, an outspoken critic of Trump, won the office in 2022 by just 280 votes after an automatic recount, making it one of the closest statewide races in Arizona history. He was competing for the nomination against Rodney Glassman, a former Democrat who was the party’s Senate nominee against John McCain in 2010 before joining the Republican party. Tuesday’s primary also settled tight contests in legislative races across Arizona, where Republicans hold slim majorities in both chambers of the state capitol.

title: US trade chief defends tariffs as Democrats say Trump ‘in denial’ over price hikes
summary: Jamieson Greer denies tariffs have pushed up prices during grilling from lawmakers over cost-of-living concerns
body: Donald Trump’s chief trade official denied the president’s sweeping tariffs have increased prices for US families, as lawmakers challenged him over cost-of-living concerns across much of the country. US inflation surged to a three-year high earlier this year. During a heated exchange before US senators on Wednesday, however, US trade representative Jamieson Greer appeared to claim Trump’s controversial economic agenda had not driven prices higher. Asked by the Democratic senator Elizabeth Warren if the tariffs had increased prices for American families, Greer said: “No.” “Core inflation fell to 2.6% year on year, much better than in January 2025,” Greer said. Core inflation excludes food and energy. Overall inflation is slightly higher than it was when Joe Biden left office. Warren said an estimate produced by Democrats and based on the nonpartisan congressional budget office and the treasury department’s own numbers, showed families were paying an average of $1,700 more in tariff costs since Trump’s return to office. It comes at a critical time for Trump’s tariffs policy, which suffered a damaging blow in February when the US supreme court ruled that many of those tariffs were illegal. The US replaced those tariffs with a 10% tariff regime on much of the world, but this is due to expire on Friday – and Trump officials, including Greer, have indicated they will replace it with a new wave of duties on imports from overseas. During Greer’s appearance before the Senate finance committee on Wednesday, Senator Raphael Warnock said Trump had “made life more expensive for everybody” before the US-Israel war on Iran sent “energy prices through the roof”. “Despite this mountain of evidence, you and the president still seem to be in denial that tariffs have raised prices,” Warnock told Greer, “a fact that study after study confirms, and is just basic common sense – everybody sees it, we’re feeling it.” Warnock asked if Greer had “ever produced any rigorous peer-evaluated study refuting the fact that the tariffs have contributed to inflation, or is your rebuttal to these conclusion just based on vibes, people’s feelings, politics … ?” Greer replied: “Well, no, we don’t do vibes, senator.” Greer began to talk about inflation and the price of eggs over the past month before Warnock pulled him up to answer his question. When Greer began talking about the price of eggs, butter and smartphones, Warnock interjected: “That’s not my question.” “The point is that tariffs contributed to inflation,” Warnock said. “Prices are higher than when the president came into office, and tariffs made it worse.”

title: Tropical storm Bertha makes landfall in Louisiana with 45mph winds
summary: In Kentucky and West Virginia, rescuers freed trapped residents as states face catastrophic floods and damage
body: Tropical storm Bertha made ‌landfall in ‌St Bernard parish, on the Gulf coast south-east of New Orleans, Louisiana, on Wednesday afternoon, the ⁠US national ⁠hurricane ⁠center said. The Miami-based forecaster ‌added that maximum sustained winds were estimated ⁠to be 45mph (75km/h) ‌with higher gusts. The weakening storm had been creeping along the coast, but still spinning off strong winds far from its center and closing beaches from Florida to Mississippi. Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season that is now unfolding. Elsewhere, a band of extreme weather that ripped through states including Kentucky and West Virginia on Tuesday night sparked devastating tornadoes and record flash flooding that left dozens of people trapped in their houses and vehicles, and injured several others. First responders in one West Virginia county were overwhelmed by the volume of calls as waters rose to unprecedented levels, with rescuers cutting through attic roofs to free those inside. Patrick Morrisey, the governor, placed all 55 counties under a state of emergency. He sent 100 national guard members to the hardest-hit areas in the northern part of the state, and an additional 200 national guard members were due to arrive on Wednesday. “State and local agencies will remain on the ground for as long as it takes to help affected communities,” Morrisey said in a statement. In Kentucky, there were reports of multiple tornadoes, flooding, landslides and widespread damage to infrastructure, including a bridge that was swept away. The threat from extreme weather conditions continued through the mid-Atlantic region on Wednesday, AccuWeather said, after damaging winds also stretched into New Jersey, New York, Ohio and Pennsylvania. “By Wednesday, the severe weather threat will shift southward, focusing on southern Delaware and southeastern Maryland into central and southeastern Virginia, and central and eastern North Carolina,” the weather outlet warned in a bulletin. Officials in Buckhannon, West Virginia, were assessing damage early on Wednesday after flooding they described as “catastrophic”. MetroNews, citing data from the National Weather Service (NWS), said the Sand Run stream crested at 10.63ft (3.24 meters) on Tuesday night, surpassing the 8.3ft mark set during floods in 1985. Local media in Upshur county reported “chaotic radio traffic” between first responders and scenes including: “People hanging from trees. Bridges destroyed. Roads washed out. Structures floating down creeks. Entire families in the water. Cars abandoned. Houses colliding into one another.” Robbie Skinner, the Buckhannon mayor, told MetroNews on Tuesday night: “I’ve never seen anything like this, and I’ve lived here for 37 years. The river is still rising, we’re not done yet.” Outlets reported that water rescue crews in swift boats were moving from property to property cutting holes in roofs to free people trapped in their attics, and dozens of people being evacuated by boat from a flooded hotel. Tens of thousands of customers were still without electricity in Ohio, Virginia and West Virginia on Wednesday morning, poweroutage.us said, with many more having been restored. Some of the worst damage from tornadoes came in Kentucky. Multiple people were injured in Grayson county, officials there said, one with serious injuries requiring hospital admission. Up to 25 buildings were severely damaged or “completely gone”, Carey Sims, the county’s director of emergency management, told WDRB News. The NWS office in Louisville said in a post on X that it planned to conduct damage surveys in at least four areas and across seven counties of the state on Wednesday morning. The Associated Press and Reuters contributed reporting

title: Zelenskyy under growing pressure to reinstate sacked defence minister
summary: Campaigners in Ukraine pledge to continue nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov gets his job back
body: Volodymyr Zelenskyy was under growing pressure to reinstate his sacked defence minister on Wednesday, after campaigners said they would continue indefinite nationwide protests until Mykhailo Fedorov got his job back. On Tuesday, Zelenskyy bowed to public demands by firing Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi. He appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new head of the country’s armed forces, as part of a radical reshuffle of the top military command. But Zelenskyy’s attempts to defuse popular anger by offering Fedorov a new government role so far appear to have failed. Fedorov has reportedly turned down the post of deputy prime minister for technological development. Instead, he wants to be reinstated as defence minister, arguing that it is the only way of implementing his ambitious plan for modernising and reforming Ukraine’s army. The post is no longer vacant after Zelenskyy appointed a special forces commander, Yevhenii Khmara, as acting minister. Protesters have called for a rally to be held on Friday evening outside Zelenskyy’s presidential office in Ivana Franka Square, Kyiv. The announcement – made by prominent veteran Dmytro Koziatynsky – suggests that Ukraine’s political crisis is likely to roll into a second week. “We should not forget about the second demand of the protest – to return Mykhailo Fedorov to the position of minister of defence,” Koziatynsky wrote on social media. He added: “We must do everything possible to ensure that the government hears and satisfies all the demands of society.” One senior army source told the Guardian that Zelenskyy faced a major dilemma. “If he reverses his decision, this would be a big victory for Fedorov. It would be humiliating for the president and reveal him to be weak. But if he doesn’t, the protests will keep going,” they said. In recent months Ukraine’s unmanned systems forces have carried out a series of long and mid-range strikes on Russian territory, as well as on occupied southern Ukraine and Crimea. Targets have included oil refineries and military depots, as well as ships and oil tankers in the Sea of Azov and Black Sea. Overnight, Ukrainian drones hit warehouses belonging to the Russian online retailer Wildberries, sparking large fires in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk. The attacks were part of an ongoing campaign to cripple Russia’s economy, so that it can no longer fund its war. Zelenskyy held a meeting on Wednesday with his top generals. Afterwards, Drapatyi wrote on Facebook that he had been given instructions to “intensify offensive operations in all domains”. He said other tasks included planning operations behind enemy lines, developing new technologies, and building up the military’s capabilities. Drapatyi’s appointment has been met with widespread approval. Ukraine’s former president Petro Poroshenko said the army trusted him. “I know him as a strategically minded, brave, and decisive man. He cherishes people. The Ukrainian people place great hopes in him,” Poroshenko said. One high-ranking military figure also welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment but said he faced significant challenges. “It won’t be easy,” they predicted. One was the likelihood that Vladimir Putin will bring in some form of mobilisation after elections in September, flooding the battlefield with new Russian recruits. The crisis in Ukraine’s military leadership was triggered by a generational conflict between Fedorov, a 35-year-old tech-savvy moderniser, and Syrskyi, a 60-year-old Soviet-trained traditionalist. Last week Zelenskyy said he had tried to mediate between the two sides, before firing Fedorov as a last resort. In a valedictory post, Syrskyi defended his record, which includes defending Kyiv and Kharkiv in the early weeks of Russia’s 2022 invasion. He said Ukraine had retaken more than 700 sq km (270 sq miles) of Ukrainian territory this year and said that he had created a separate drone branch of the armed forces – the first of its kind in the world. “I am handing over to my successor an army that is not only holding the defence but is also on the offensive – with initiative, with structure, with people who know how to beat the enemy,” he said. He added that he sincerely hoped the offensive would continue, adding: “Everything is in place for this.” As part of his shake-up, Zelenskyy said ⁠major general ⁠Ihor Skybiuk would replace ⁠Andrii Hnatov as the head of ⁠the general staff. ‌The president described Skybiuk as a “highly experienced officer who defended Ukraine shoulder ‌to shoulder with the new commander-in-chief.” Russian bloggers have acknowledged that Drapatyi is a formidable opponent. “He’s smart, strong, and hates Russia,” one remarked. They also noted that he had risen from battalion commander to head of the armed forces – a career which is practically impossible in Russia, where promotions are decided on the basis of connections and “intrigue”. The Kremlin said on Wednesday that Drapatyi’s appointment would not alter the situation on the frontline. “I don’t think this will lead to any changes,” Putin’s spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said, adding that Moscow has no intention of stopping its full-scale offensive, which has lasted for nearly four and a half years.

title: London loses again: FTSE 100 landlord Segro will be missed
summary: Doubly depressing takeover by a US buyer deprives the UK market of a property company that is genuinely different
body: For a few hours on Wednesday, it seemed possible we were about to witness a rare stock market event: a FTSE 100 company holding out against a hostile raider from the US and defying some of its own large shareholders to defend its independence. Sadly, it didn’t happen. Segro, the FTSE 100 warehouse landlord known as Slough Estates for much of its corporate life, capitulated minutes before the deadline and said it was “minded to recommend” the “best and final” offer of £14bn, or £10.32 a share, from the US giant Prologis of San Francisco. The two sides now have until 12 August to hammer out a firm agreement. The deal will be the biggest Footsie takeover so far in the current bid-heavy year. In a couple of ways, it will also be the most depressing. First, because David Sleath, Segro’s long-serving chief executive, put up a decent fight and had the better of the arguments. While most property transactions happen close to the book value of the assets (905p in this case), Sleath invited Segro’s shareholders to lie back, be patient and think of the growth opportunities in AI datacentres and big-box warehouses for online retailers and suchlike. “A unique portfolio focused on Europe’s most supply-constrained markets,” went the argument. Segro cited an estimate from CBRE, the commercial property investment firm, of a near-£18bn valuation, or £13 a share, within a few years on a standalone basis thanks to a boost from datacentre expansion. Prologis’s argument, in essence, was that such valuations were unrealistic because Segro lacks the financial muscle to make the most of the opportunities. Its pitch to shareholders was to take the money – or, rather, accept the terms of the share swap since the cash element of the offer, injected late in the day, is only 25%. The 14% bid premium to the last asset valuation was enough to get some big Segro shareholders salivating. Led by Norway’s sovereign wealth fund, with an 8% stake in Segro, the calls for “engagement” had grown louder in recent days. The tale is wearyingly familiar. Even when boards are up for a real scrap (which isn’t always the case), the dead hand of institutional money intervenes. In this case, there is an added sense of what might have been because most of the investors calling for a deal also had holdings in Prologis, which has a $135bn (£101bn) market capitalisation. For those international investors with a foot in both camps, the quarrel over fair terms will almost have been a spreadsheet exercise in portfolio management. That doesn’t make a properly fair fight. The second depressing feature is that, post-Segro, London’s real estate sector looks denuded. The company is the biggest listed commercial landlord by a distance and is genuinely different. Here’s Panmure Liberum’s analyst Bjorn Zietsman in a recent note: “Segro is one of a small number of listed, pure play vehicles offering direct exposure to UK and European datacentre and logistics development. “If Segro is absorbed into Prologis, that exposure gets absorbed and the capital allocation decision behind it disappears. Investors lose the ability to choose UK/European datacentre and logistics growth specifically, and instead inherit whatever weighting Prologis’s management chooses to give the UK and Europe within a global platform spanning 20 countries and £200bn of combined assets under management.” In other words, a small piece of diversity is lost from the London stock market, at least in the property sector. You’ll still find plenty of real estate investment trusts offering the usual bland mix of London office blocks and regional shopping centres, but multi-decade pan-European plays on AI datacentres are harder to come by. Prologis’s promise to get a secondary listing in London is not a consolation: we know from experience that most of those add-on listings don’t last because trading in the shares inevitably gravitates to the US. The loss of the company that began life as the Slough Trading Company in 1920 probably won’t register on UK political radars, but it ought to. This takeover is another entry in the hollowing-out of the UK stock market, which has become an easy hunting ground for overseas firms with richer valuations. One would grumble less if the take-out prices were other-worldly. In Segro’s case, though, the terms look only so-so if one takes a long-term view. The outcome could have been different. This is (another) bad one to lose.

title: Tories ask HMRC to investigate whether Nigel Farage owes tax on £5m gift
summary: Party chair queries status of funds Reform leader received from crypto billionaire Christopher Harborne
body: The Conservatives have asked HMRC to investigate whether Nigel Farage should have paid tax on the £5m gift he received from the crypto billionaire Christopher Harborne. The party chair, Kevin Hollinrake, wrote to the tax authorities questioning whether it was a payment to compensate him for running to be an MP. Farage has said the gift was purely personal and unconditional, with a document drawn up to say it was given with no expectation in return. He has also previously said the payment was to go towards his personal security costs, and a reward for campaigning for Brexit. He said it was no one’s business what he did with the money and that he could “spend it on Ferraris” if he wanted. The Guardian, however, reported this month that three sources had said Farage informed senior Reform UK figures that he would require compensation of £1m per year to offset his loss of earnings if he were to run for office. A Reform spokesperson said at the time that the Guardian’s report was “fake and wholly incorrect”. Lawyers for Harborne referred back to his previous statements about the gift, saying he “expected nothing in return” and “did not envisage Mr Farage returning to politics”. In light of that reporting, Hollinrake has written to HMRC asking it to investigate. “If those reports are accurate, they raise legitimate questions as to whether the payment was truly a personal gift, or whether it was connected to Mr Farage resuming the leadership of Reform UK and standing for election to parliament,” he wrote. “If the payment was connected to discussions about Mr Farage returning as leader of Reform UK, and standing for parliament with a view to becoming a member of parliament, there would appear to be a serious question as to whether it should properly be characterised as income rather than a gift. “I believe it is imperative that HMRC examines whether the payment falls within the ‘disguised remuneration’ regime or otherwise constitutes taxable earnings arising from an office, employment or political position. “If the payment was intended as a reward, inducement or compensation connected with Mr Farage’s roles within Reform UK, the tax treatment may be materially different from that applicable to a genuine personal gift and different to the tax status of a normal political donation (not that it was declared as such).” The tax expert Dan Neidle has already raised questions around whether the £5m should have incurred tax after the Guardian’s reporting. Reform UK has been approached for comment. Farage faces a standards inquiry over Harborne’s gift and other undeclared funding from the fraudster George Cottrell. He told the Triggernometry podcast over the weekend that the £5m was “totally undeclarable in every single way”. He acknowledged that the parliamentary standards system could find against him, but claimed he had sought advice from a top international lawyer who told him the £5m did not need to be declared because it was unconditional and he had a document saying it had been given freely. The rules state that gifts and benefits in kind have to be declared for the 12 months before an MP enters parliament, but only if they are political. Farage is fighting a byelection against more than 30 candidates in Clacton, but none of them are from the main parties, which decided to boycott the contest. He could potentially face a second byelection if the commissioner finds against him and the standards committee recommends a substantial sanction including suspension from the Commons, triggering a possible recall petition. The major parties have said they would stand against him in a second contest were it to occur.

title: Prosecutors argue D4vd had financial motive to kill Celeste Rivas Hernandez
summary: Details emerge about how much Interscope Records paid the singer at hearing to determine if there is probable cause for murder case to proceed to trial
body: Singer D4vd had a multimillion-dollar recording deal, an executive at his former label testified on Wednesday, as prosecutors sought to establish the musician had a financial motivation to kill Celeste Rivas Hernandez. The scope of how much Interscope Records was paying the singer, born David Anthony Burke, came into clear view on the second day of a hearing to determine whether there was probable cause for him to stand trial for the murder and sexual abuse of Rivas Hernandez. The label terminated his contract in December 2025. The teenager’s body was found last year in gruesome conditions in a car registered to the musician. Prosecutors accuse Burke of stabbing Rivas Hernandez, 14, to death at his Los Angeles residence to silence her from divulging the sexual nature of their relationship as his career was on the rise. Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges that include first-degree murder, “lewd and lascivious acts” with a child under the age of 14, and the mutilation of human remains. His defense team has said their client “was not the cause” of Hernandez’s death. From 2022 to 2025, Interscope paid Burke and affiliated entities more than $7.2m, a figure that encompassed legal fees and a trust mandated by California law for underage entertainers, according to a summary of payments exhibited to the courtroom. Details of Burke’s compensation followed a testy exchange in the morning, as prosecutors countered the defense’s suggestion that their client was not privy to his finances. Attorneys for Burke repeatedly objected to questions from deputy district attorney Beth Silverman about whether the singer’s former financial manager, Benjamin Gregor, knew of Burke arranging tickets for Rivas Hernandez to travel to London and his home in Texas. Rivas Hernandez’s parents, Jesus Rivas and Mercedes Martinez, returned to court on Wednesday. Dressed in all black, the two sat in the first row, accompanied by their lawyer. Martinez dropped her head and wiped her eyes in the early afternoon, when prosecutors presented a photograph of her daughter’s passport, which a Caltrans employee discovered in January 2026 in a dirt embankment off State Route 154 in Santa Barbara. “Once he found the card, he Googled the name to try and see if it was someone that lived locally in the area. He began seeing articles and news stories and when he realized what he had found, he called our dispatch,” Sean Buenrostro, a California highway patrol officer, said on the stand. The first day of hearings on Tuesday featured graphic evidence. Prosecutors showed photos of the dismembered and decomposing body of Rivas Hernandez, and alleged the singer had severely mutilated her body. They also sought to show that the singer used his credit cards to purchase chainsaws, a body bag and a shovel. On Wednesday, Chelsea Murillo, a criminalist with the Los Angeles police department’s forensic science division, spoke about her examination of the Tesla that contained Rivas Hernandez’s remains. A layer of dust coated the vehicle’s exterior, she said, which suggested it had been outside for a long period of time. She observed insect activity in the front trunk and a strong odor resembling a decomposing body. The preliminary hearing is expected to last from three to five days. Burke, now 21, was arrested in April and has been held in custody since then without bail. If convicted of murder, he could face the death penalty, or life in prison without the possibility of parole. Prosecutors have not yet announced whether they will pursue capital punishment. After Burke’s arrest in April, the family said in a statement: “Celeste was a beautiful, strong girl who loved to sing and dance. Every Friday night was movie night and we spent wonderful times together. We love her very much and she always told us that she loved us. We miss her deeply. All we want is justice for Celeste.” The Associated Press contributed to this report
